concerns.text: The plan validates the correlation results on a held-out period (2018-2020) using the SAME autocorrelation-adjusted p-values derived from the training set (1998-2017). The effective sample size (Neff) and the lag-1 autocorrelation (ρ1) are calculated on the 'full continuous time series' (FR-010). If Neff is computed on the full series, the p-values are not independent of the training data's temporal structure. A true out-of-sample validation requires re-estimating the autocorrelation structure (ρ1) and Neff specifically on the test period to verify if the statistical significance holds under the test period's specific noise characteristics. Using training-derived Neff for test validation creates a circular dependency where the 'validation' is merely a projection of the training set's statistical properties.

The validation strategy splits data into Train (1998-2017) and Test (2018-2020) but the methodology (FR-010) mandates calculating Neff on the 'full continuous time series'. This makes the 'validation' step scientifically invalid as a test of generalizability. The p-values for the test set are mathematically determined by the training set's autocorrelation. The analysis cannot return a negative result for the test set based on statistical significance if the significance threshold was derived from the training set's properties. The plan must either: (a) calculate Neff separately for the test period, or (b) reframe the validation as a simple correlation coefficient check without re-applying the p-value significance test derived from the full series.

The plan applies a single global Bonferroni correction (α/30) to the entire set of 30 hypotheses. However, the validation phase (US-3) implies checking if specific pairs exceed |r| > 0.5 in the test set. If the test set is treated as a separate hypothesis testing environment, the multiple comparison correction should ideally be applied within the test set context or the test set should be used for effect size estimation only, not significance testing. Applying a global correction derived from the full dataset to a subset (test set) without adjusting for the reduced sample size or re-evaluating the family-wise error rate for the specific test window is methodologically weak for a 'validation' claim.
